No, because the essential exception applies only when the particular visual presentation itself is essential to the information; here only the wording is mandated, so HTML text with CSS must be used (1.4.5)
No, because 1.4.5 prohibits all images of text on websites that support CSS, with the only exception being logotypes
explanation: WCAG 1.4.5's essential exception permits images of text only when a particular presentation of text is essential to the information being conveyed, such as a logotype or a typeface sample. Here, the regulatory body mandates the wording, not the visual formatting; the disclaimer's meaning is fully conveyed through HTML text styled with CSS. The first option conflates legally required content with legally required visual presentation. The third option incorrectly narrows 1.4.5's exceptions to logotypes only, ignoring the Customizable exception and the broader scope of the Essential exception.
learning_objective: Distinguish between content that is legally required (wording) and visual presentation that is essential (specific formatting), recognizing that only the latter triggers the essential exception in 1.4.5.

wcag_extract: The visual presentation of the following have a contrast ratio of at least 3:1 against adjacent color(s): User Interface Components (visual information required to identify user interface components and states, except for inactive components or where the appearance of the component is determined by the user agent and not modified by the author); Graphical Objects (parts of graphics required to understand the content, except when a particular presentation of graphics is essential to the information being conveyed).
question: A web application has a custom toggle switch. In its 'off' state, the track is medium gray on a white background (4:1 contrast). In its 'on' state, the track turns to a light green that has only 2.2:1 contrast against the same white background. The toggle's label text reads 'Notifications' and meets text contrast requirements. A tester flags the 'on' state as a 1.4.11 failure. The developer argues that the label makes the state clear. Who is correct?
choices: The developer is correct; as long as the component's label clearly communicates the state, the visual appearance of the toggle track does not need to meet 1.4.11
Neither; the toggle state is communicated programmatically through ARIA attributes, so the visual contrast of state indicators is governed by 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value, not 1.4.11
The tester is correct; 1.4.11 requires the visual information needed to identify component states to meet 3:1 contrast, and the 'on' state's track color is that visual information
explanation: WCAG 1.4.11 explicitly requires 3:1 contrast for the visual information needed to identify user interface components and their states. The toggle track's color change is the visual indicator of the 'on' versus 'off' state; at 2.2:1 in the 'on' state, it fails. The first option incorrectly assumes a text label substitutes for visual state contrast; 1.4.11 addresses the visual presentation itself. The second option conflates visual contrast requirements with programmatic state exposure; 4.1.2 ensures assistive technologies can read the state, but 1.4.11 ensures sighted users can perceive it visually. Both criteria apply independently.
learning_objective: Recognize that 1.4.11 covers component states (not just component identification), and that programmatic state exposure (4.1.2) does not exempt the visual presentation from meeting the 3:1 contrast threshold.

wcag_extract: In content implemented using markup languages that support the following text style properties, no loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following and by changing no other style property: line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size; spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size; letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size; word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size.
question: A web page uses HTML and CSS. A tester applies only increased letter spacing (0.12em) and word spacing (0.16em) without changing line height or paragraph spacing. Some button labels now overflow their containers. The tester reports a 1.4.12 failure. The developer argues the test was performed incorrectly because not all four properties were changed simultaneously. Who is correct?
choices: The developer is correct; 1.4.12 requires all four spacing values to be set together, so testing individual properties in isolation is not a valid conformance test
The tester is correct; 1.4.12 must be met for each property individually, and any content loss from changing a single listed property is a failure
Neither; 1.4.12 only requires that authors do not use CSS to prevent users from overriding spacing, regardless of whether content adapts
explanation: WCAG 1.4.12 states 'no loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following and by changing no other style property.' The normative text specifies that all four properties are applied simultaneously as a set. A test that modifies only a subset does not match the criterion's conditions. The second option misreads the criterion as requiring per-property compliance, which could yield different results since the combined effect of all four changes is what the criterion measures. The third option incorrectly reduces 1.4.12 to a non-interference requirement; the criterion requires content to survive specific spacing values, not merely that authors refrain from blocking overrides.
learning_objective: Understand that 1.4.12's conformance test requires applying all four spacing properties simultaneously; testing individual properties in isolation does not constitute a valid 1.4.12 evaluation.

wcag_extract: Where receiving and then removing pointer hover or keyboard focus triggers additional content to become visible and then hidden, the following are true: Dismissible (a mechanism is available to dismiss the additional content without moving pointer hover or keyboard focus, unless the additional content communicates an input error or does not obscure or replace other content); Hoverable (if pointer hover can trigger the additional content, then the pointer can be moved over the additional content without the additional content disappearing); Persistent (the additional content remains visible until the hover or focus trigger is removed, the user dismisses it, or its information is no longer valid).
question: A help icon next to a form field shows a popover when hovered. The popover is positioned in empty space and does not cover any other content or controls. There is no way to dismiss it with Escape; it only disappears when the pointer leaves the trigger icon. The pointer can move over the popover without it vanishing. Does this meet 1.4.13?
choices: No, because every hover-triggered popover must be dismissible via a mechanism like Escape, regardless of whether it obscures other content
No, because the popover disappears only when hover is removed, which violates the Persistent requirement since the user cannot control its duration
Yes, because the Dismissible requirement has an exception when the additional content does not obscure or replace other content, and the Hoverable and Persistent requirements are both met
explanation: WCAG 1.4.13's Dismissible requirement includes an exception: a dismiss mechanism is not required when the additional content does not obscure or replace other content. Since the popover sits in empty space and covers nothing, this exception applies. The Hoverable requirement is met because the pointer can move over the popover. The Persistent requirement is met because the content remains visible until the hover trigger is removed. The first option ignores the 'does not obscure or replace' exception in the Dismissible clause. The second option misreads Persistent; the content remaining until hover is removed is exactly what Persistent requires.
learning_objective: Identify the 'does not obscure or replace other content' exception within 1.4.13's Dismissible requirement, recognizing that non-obscuring hover content is exempt from needing a separate dismiss mechanism.

wcag_extract: If keyboard focus can be moved to a component of the page using a keyboard interface, then focus can be moved away from that component using only a keyboard interface, and, if it requires more than unmodified arrow or tab keys or other standard exit methods, the user is advised of the method for moving focus away.
question: A date picker widget captures arrow keys for navigating between days in the calendar grid. A user can exit the date picker by pressing Tab, which moves focus to the next form field. No special instructions are displayed because Tab is the standard method. Does this conform to 2.1.2?
choices: Yes, because focus can be moved away using Tab, which is a standard exit method, and 2.1.2 only requires user notification when non-standard methods are needed
No, because capturing arrow keys within the date picker constitutes a partial keyboard trap that violates 2.1.2, since all keys must pass through freely
No, because 2.1.2 requires that users be advised of any keyboard interaction pattern that differs from default browser behavior, including the captured arrow keys
explanation: WCAG 2.1.2 requires that focus can be moved away from any component using only a keyboard interface. Since Tab (a standard exit method) moves focus out of the date picker, the exit requirement is satisfied. The criterion only requires advising the user when moving focus away requires more than unmodified arrow or tab keys or other standard exit methods. Capturing arrow keys for internal navigation within a widget is a standard interaction pattern for composite widgets and does not constitute a keyboard trap, as long as a standard key like Tab can move focus out. The second option confuses internal key handling with trapping. The third option expands the notification requirement beyond what the normative text states.
learning_objective: Recognize that internal key capture within a composite widget (e.g., arrow keys in a date picker) does not violate 2.1.2 as long as a standard exit method like Tab moves focus away, with no special instructions needed.

wcag_extract: If a keyboard shortcut is implemented in content using only letter (including upper- and lower-case letters), punctuation, number, or symbol characters, then at least one of the following is true: Turn off (a mechanism is available to turn the shortcut off); Remap (a mechanism is available to remap the shortcut to include one or more non-printable keyboard keys); Active only on focus (the keyboard shortcut for a user interface component is only active when that component has focus).
question: A social media web app uses the '/' key as a global shortcut to jump focus to the search bar from anywhere on the page. The shortcut is always active regardless of which component has focus. There is a settings page where users can disable all keyboard shortcuts. A speech recognition user reports that dictating words with a slash-like sound triggers the search bar. Does this implementation satisfy 2.1.4?
choices: No, because global single-character shortcuts must be remappable to include a modifier key; a turn-off option alone is insufficient under 2.1.4
Yes, because the settings page provides a mechanism to turn off the shortcut, which satisfies one of the three conditions in 2.1.4
No, because '/' is a navigation key in some browsers (e.g., Firefox quick-find), so intercepting it violates 2.1.1 Keyboard rather than 2.1.4
explanation: WCAG 2.1.4 requires that single-character shortcuts meet at least one of three conditions: turn off, remap, or active only on focus. The '/' key is a printable character used without a modifier, so 2.1.4 applies. Since the settings page offers a mechanism to turn off the shortcut, the Turn off condition is satisfied. The first option incorrectly requires remapping specifically; 2.1.4 uses an 'at least one of' structure, and any single condition suffices. The third option conflates browser-level key handling with WCAG 2.1.1 keyboard operability; while intercepting '/' may cause usability friction in Firefox, the WCAG criterion at issue is 2.1.4, and the turn-off mechanism addresses it.
learning_objective: Understand that 2.1.4's three conditions (turn off, remap, active only on focus) are alternatives; satisfying any one of them is sufficient for conformance.

wcag_extract: For each time limit that is set by the content, at least one of the following is true: Turn off, Adjust, Extend, Real-time Exception, Essential Exception, 20 Hour Exception.
question: A live online auction site gives bidders exactly 30 seconds to place each bid after the previous bid is received. There is no option to extend, pause, or adjust this countdown. The auction operator says the time limit is essential to the competitive nature of the auction. A tester evaluates this against 2.2.1. Which exception, if any, applies?
choices: The Essential Exception applies, because the 30-second bidding window is essential to the competitive nature of the auction and extending it would fundamentally change the activity
The Real-time Exception applies, because the auction is a real-time event where the time limit is a required part of the activity, making 2.2.1 inapplicable
No exception applies; regardless of the competitive nature, 2.2.1 requires at least a warning and extension mechanism for any countdown under 20 hours
explanation: WCAG 2.2.1 includes a Real-time Exception for situations where the time limit is a required part of a real-time event (such as an auction) and no alternative to the time limit is possible. A live auction is the canonical example of a real-time event; the countdown is inherent to the auction format. The Essential Exception (first option) is a separate provision that covers cases where the time limit is essential and extending it would invalidate the activity; while conceptually similar, the Real-time Exception is the more precise fit for live events with inherent timing. The third option ignores both exceptions entirely, misreading 2.2.1 as having no carve-outs for real-time activities.
learning_objective: Distinguish the Real-time Exception from the Essential Exception in 2.2.1, recognizing that live events like auctions where timing is inherent to the format fall under the Real-time Exception.

wcag_extract: For moving, blinking, scrolling, or auto-updating information, all of the following are true: Moving, blinking, scrolling; for any moving, blinking or scrolling information that starts automatically, lasts more than five seconds, and is presented in parallel with other content, there is a mechanism for the user to pause, stop, or hide it unless the movement, blinking, or scrolling is part of an activity where it is essential. Auto-updating; for any auto-updating information that starts automatically and is presented in parallel with other content, there is a mechanism for the user to pause, stop, or hide it or to control the frequency of the update unless the auto-updating is part of an activity where it is essential.
question: A loading spinner animation plays for 3 seconds while content is being fetched. During this time, no other content is displayed on the page; the spinner is the only visible element. Once loading completes, the spinner is replaced by the actual page content. A tester flags this as a 2.2.2 violation because there is no pause button for the animation. Is the tester correct?
choices: Yes, because any animation that starts automatically requires a pause mechanism under 2.2.2, regardless of duration or context
No, but only because loading spinners are considered essential to the activity of indicating progress, which triggers the essential exception
No, because the spinner lasts only 3 seconds, which is under the 5-second threshold for moving content, and it is not presented in parallel with other content
explanation: WCAG 2.2.2's moving/blinking/scrolling clause has three conditions that must all be true to trigger the requirement: the content starts automatically, lasts more than five seconds, and is presented in parallel with other content. This spinner fails two of those conditions: it lasts only 3 seconds (under the 5-second threshold) and is not presented alongside other content (it is the sole element on screen). Either condition alone would take it out of scope. The first option ignores both the duration threshold and the parallel-content requirement. The second option misapplies the essential exception; loading indicators may be useful, but the spinner is already outside scope without needing an exception.
learning_objective: Apply both the five-second duration threshold and the parallel-content condition in 2.2.2, recognizing that moving content must meet all stated conditions before the pause/stop/hide requirement is triggered.

wcag_extract: Web pages do not contain anything that flashes more than three times in any one second period, or the flash is below the general flash and red flash thresholds.
question: A web application includes a user-uploaded video that contains a scene with rapid strobe effects flashing 6 times per second. The site's terms of service prohibit users from uploading flashing content, but there is no automated detection or server-side mitigation. A compliance reviewer asks whether the page conforms to 2.3.1. The site operator says user-generated content is outside the scope of WCAG conformance. Is the operator correct?
choices: No, because the page contains the flashing content regardless of who uploaded it; the web page as a whole must not contain anything that flashes above the threshold, and terms-of-service alone do not ensure conformance
Yes, because WCAG conformance only applies to author-created content; user-uploaded videos are outside the author's control and therefore exempt from 2.3.1
No, but the issue is 1.2.5 Audio Description rather than 2.3.1, because the problem is with video content that lacks sufficient alternative descriptions
explanation: WCAG 2.3.1 states that web pages do not contain anything that flashes more than three times in any one second period (or below threshold). The criterion applies to the web page as delivered to the user, not to who authored the content. If user-uploaded video displays on the page with 6 flashes per second, the page fails. A terms-of-service prohibition without technical enforcement does not prevent the flashing from occurring. The second option incorrectly narrows WCAG scope to exclude hosted user content. The third option misidentifies the criterion entirely; audio description (1.2.5) addresses providing narration for video, which is unrelated to seizure-inducing flash rates.
learning_objective: Understand that 2.3.1 applies to the entire web page as experienced by users, including hosted user-generated content, and that policy-only controls without technical enforcement do not ensure conformance.

wcag_extract: A mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple web pages.
question: A documentation site has 50 pages, each with the same sidebar containing 40 navigation links. Every page uses proper heading structure (h1 for the page title, h2 for sections) but has no skip link and no ARIA landmarks. The developer argues that the heading structure lets screen reader users jump directly to the h1, effectively bypassing the sidebar. Does this satisfy 2.4.1?
choices: No, because 2.4.1 specifically requires either a skip link or ARIA landmarks; heading structure alone is not a recognized bypass mechanism
No, because heading navigation is only available to screen reader users, and 2.4.1 requires a mechanism that benefits sighted keyboard users as well
Yes, because heading structure is a recognized technique for bypassing repeated blocks under 2.4.1, and the criterion does not prescribe a specific mechanism
explanation: WCAG 2.4.1 requires 'a mechanism' to bypass repeated blocks but does not mandate any specific technique. The WCAG sufficient techniques include using heading elements to mark sections (technique H69), ARIA landmarks, and skip links, among others. A well-structured heading hierarchy allows users with screen readers to jump past repeated navigation to the main content heading. The first option incorrectly limits acceptable techniques to skip links and landmarks. The second option adds a requirement that the mechanism benefit all user groups equally, which is not stated in the normative text; the criterion requires that a mechanism is available, and heading navigation qualifies as one such mechanism.
learning_objective: Recognize that 2.4.1 is technique-agnostic and that heading structure is a valid bypass mechanism, not limited to skip links or ARIA landmarks.

wcag_extract: Web pages have titles that describe topic or purpose.
question: A university website opens search results in a new browser tab. The new tab's <title> reads "Search" regardless of the query entered. A student searches for "financial aid" and the resulting page title is just "Search". The developer argues the title is adequate because it identifies the page type. Which statement best reflects the 2.4.2 requirement?
choices: The title satisfies 2.4.2 because it describes the purpose of the page — displaying search results — even without including the query terms

The title fails 2.4.2 because page titles must describe the topic or purpose; a bare "Search" title on a results page does not convey what was searched for, making it inadequate when multiple search tabs are open
The title fails because 2.4.2 requires the page title to include the site name followed by the page purpose in a specific format, and omitting the site name is the actual violation
explanation: WCAG 2.4.2 requires page titles that describe topic or purpose. While 'Search' identifies the page type, it does not describe the topic — the specific query or result set. When a user has multiple tabs open, indistinguishable titles prevent orientation. Option A is wrong because merely naming the page type is not sufficient when the title fails to distinguish the content from other search result pages. Option C is wrong because 2.4.2 does not prescribe a specific format or mandate site-name inclusion; the requirement is descriptiveness, not a formatting rule.
learning_objective: Understand that 2.4.2 requires titles to describe both topic and purpose, and that a page-type label alone is insufficient when it fails to distinguish the page's specific content.

wcag_extract: The purpose of each link can be determined from the link text alone or from the link text together with its programmatically determined link context, except where the purpose of the link would be ambiguous to users in general.
question:
A travel booking site has a link that reads "Click here" inside a paragraph: "For our cancellation policy, click here." The paragraph is in a
with no ARIA roles or semantic markup — it is not inside a

,

  • , or any element that provides programmatically determined context for the link. A developer says the surrounding sentence makes the purpose clear. Does this pass 2.4.4?
choices: Yes, because the visual proximity of the sentence is sufficient context under 2.4.4; programmatic determination is only required at the AAA level under 2.4.9
Yes, because 2.4.4's ambiguity exception applies — users in general would understand 'click here' in the context of the visible sentence
No, because 2.4.4 requires context to be programmatically determined, and text in a non-semantic <div> may not provide programmatically determined link context as defined by the criterion
explanation: WCAG 2.4.4 specifies that link purpose must be determinable from the link text alone or from the link text together with its 'programmatically determined link context.' The criterion defines this as context available through structure — such as being in the same paragraph, list item, or table cell, or via aria-labelledby. A bare <div> without semantic meaning may not establish programmatically determined context. Option A is wrong because 2.4.4 explicitly mentions programmatic determination at Level A, not just at AAA. Option B misapplies the ambiguity exception, which covers links whose destination is genuinely ambiguous to all users (e.g., a 'mystery link' game), not links with vague text in a visually nearby sentence.
learning_objective: Recognize that 2.4.4's 'programmatically determined link context' depends on proper semantic markup, not just visual proximity, and correctly scope the ambiguity exception.

wcag_extract: More than one way is available to locate a web page within a set of web pages except where the web page is the result of, or a step in, a process.
question: A recipe website has 500 recipe pages. Users can find any recipe through the main navigation categories (e.g., Desserts > Cakes). The site also has a search function. However, individual recipe pages have no related-recipe links, no breadcrumb trail, and no sitemap. A tester says the site fails 2.4.5. Is the tester correct?
choices: No, because 2.4.5 requires more than one way to locate a page, and the site provides two: category navigation and search
Yes, because 2.4.5 requires at least three independent navigation methods (navigation, search, and sitemap) for sites with more than 100 pages
Yes, because 2.4.5 requires that every page be reachable through related content links in addition to any menu-based navigation
explanation: WCAG 2.4.5 requires 'more than one way' to locate a web page within a set of web pages. The criterion does not specify a minimum number beyond two, nor does it mandate particular methods like sitemaps or related-content links. Category navigation plus search satisfies the requirement. Option B is wrong because 2.4.5 does not scale its requirements based on site size or require three methods. Option C is wrong because the criterion does not mandate any specific navigation method; any combination of two or more is acceptable.
learning_objective: Understand that 2.4.5 requires at least two ways to locate a page but does not mandate specific navigation methods or scale requirements by site size.

wcag_extract: Headings and labels describe topic or purpose.
question: An analytics dashboard has four chart sections. Each section has a heading: "Revenue Overview", "User Engagement", "Conversion Funnel", and "Traffic Sources". Below each chart is a filter labeled "Filter". A tester says the headings pass 2.4.6 but the filter labels fail it. The developer argues that since each filter is visually positioned under its chart, the context makes "Filter" descriptive enough. Who is correct?
choices: The developer is correct; labels inherit context from nearby headings, so a generic 'Filter' label is descriptive when placed under a specific chart heading
The tester is correct; 2.4.6 requires that labels themselves describe topic or purpose, and 'Filter' alone does not indicate what is being filtered — regardless of visual positioning
Neither is correct; 2.4.6 applies only to headings, not to form labels, so the filter labels are outside the criterion's scope entirely
explanation: WCAG 2.4.6 requires both headings and labels to describe topic or purpose. The word 'Filter' alone does not communicate what the filter controls — revenue data, engagement metrics, etc. Visual positioning does not change whether the label itself is descriptive. Option A is wrong because 2.4.6 evaluates each label on its own descriptiveness, not on inherited spatial context. Option C is wrong because 2.4.6 explicitly covers labels in addition to headings; the normative text reads 'Headings and labels describe topic or purpose.'
learning_objective: Recognize that 2.4.6 evaluates each label's descriptiveness independently, and that visual context from nearby headings does not make a generic label compliant.

wcag_extract: Any keyboard operable user interface has a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator is visible.
question: A design system applies `outline: none` globally but adds a visible 3px blue border on `:focus-visible` (triggered by keyboard navigation). During mouse clicks, no focus indicator appears. An accessibility consultant says this fails 2.4.7 because focus indicators must be visible during all interaction modes. Is the consultant correct?
choices: Yes, because 2.4.7 requires the focus indicator to be visible whenever any element receives focus, including via mouse clicks and programmatic focus
Yes, because 2.4.7 prohibits using `outline: none` under any circumstances; the browser default focus style must always be preserved
No, because 2.4.7 requires 'a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator is visible,' and showing indicators during keyboard use via :focus-visible satisfies this
explanation: WCAG 2.4.7 uses the phrase 'a mode of operation where the keyboard focus indicator is visible.' This means there must be a way for keyboard users to see focus — it does not require focus indicators during mouse interaction. The :focus-visible approach, which shows indicators during keyboard navigation but not mouse clicks, is a valid technique. Option A overstates the requirement by demanding visibility across all interaction modes. Option B is wrong because 2.4.7 does not mandate browser defaults; custom focus styles are acceptable as long as they are visible during keyboard operation.
learning_objective: Understand the 'mode of operation' language in 2.4.7 and that suppressing focus indicators during mouse use while preserving them for keyboard use is a compliant approach.

wcag_extract: When a user interface component receives keyboard focus, the component is not entirely hidden due to author-created content.
question: A web application has a slide-out notification panel that overlays the left 300px of the page. When the panel is open and a user tabs to a sidebar link behind it, the link is 100% hidden by the panel. However, the panel auto-closes after 8 seconds, at which point the focused link becomes visible. The developer argues that the temporary nature of the panel means 2.4.11 is met. Is this argument valid?
choices: No, because 2.4.11 requires that a focused component is not entirely hidden at the moment it receives focus; a delayed reveal does not satisfy the criterion
Yes, because 2.4.11 includes an exception for content that automatically dismisses itself within a reasonable timeframe
No, but the applicable criterion is 2.4.7 Focus Visible, not 2.4.11, because the issue is about the focus indicator being hidden rather than the component itself
explanation: WCAG 2.4.11 requires that when a component receives keyboard focus, it is not entirely hidden due to author-created content. The criterion contains no exception for temporary overlays or auto-dismissing panels — the requirement applies at the moment focus is received. Option B is wrong because 2.4.11 has no time-based exception. Option C is wrong because the issue here is the component itself being hidden behind the panel, not merely the focus indicator being invisible — that distinction is what separates 2.4.11 from 2.4.7.
learning_objective: Understand that 2.4.11 has no exception for temporarily obscuring content and that the criterion applies at the moment focus is received, regardless of whether the obstruction later resolves.

wcag_extract: All functionality that uses multipoint or path-based gestures for operation can be operated with a single pointer without a path-based gesture, unless a multipoint or path-based gesture is essential.
question: A data visualization dashboard lets users draw a lasso (freeform circle) around chart data points to select them. There is also a "Select Range" button that opens a dialog where users can specify minimum and maximum values to select points numerically. A tester says the lasso is a path-based gesture that violates 2.5.1. Is the tester correct?
choices: Yes, because 2.5.1 prohibits path-based gestures entirely; the lasso must be removed even though an alternative exists
No, because the lasso selects discrete points, making it a series of single-pointer taps rather than a path-based gesture under 2.5.1
No, because the Select Range dialog provides a single-pointer alternative that achieves the same functionality without a path-based gesture, satisfying 2.5.1
explanation: WCAG 2.5.1 requires that functionality using path-based gestures can also be operated with a single pointer without a path-based gesture. It does not prohibit path-based gestures — it requires alternatives. The Select Range dialog serves as that alternative. Option A is wrong because 2.5.1 does not ban path-based gestures; it mandates alternatives. Option B is wrong because a lasso is indeed a path-based gesture — the path shape determines which points are selected, not individual tap endpoints.
learning_objective: Understand that 2.5.1 requires single-pointer alternatives to path-based gestures, not elimination of those gestures, and recognize that lasso selection is a path-based interaction.

wcag_extract: For functionality that can be operated using a single pointer, at least one of the following is true: No Down-Event (the down-event of the pointer is not used to execute any part of the function); Abort or Undo (completion of the function is on the up-event, and a mechanism is available to abort the function before completion or to undo the function after completion); Up Reversal (the up-event reverses any outcome of the preceding down-event); Essential (completing the function on the down-event is essential).
question: A drag-and-drop file manager highlights the drop target on mousedown and moves the file on mouseup. If the user releases the pointer outside any valid drop target, the file returns to its original position. A tester flags this as a 2.5.2 failure because the down-event initiates visual feedback (highlighting). Does this fail 2.5.2?
choices: Yes, because the down-event is used to execute part of the function (highlighting the drag), which violates the 'No Down-Event' requirement of 2.5.2
No, because 2.5.2 only applies to click/tap interactions, not to drag-and-drop operations, which are governed by 2.5.7 Dragging Movements
No, because the function completes on the up-event and can be aborted by releasing outside a valid target; this satisfies the 'Abort or Undo' provision of 2.5.2
explanation: WCAG 2.5.2 provides four alternative paths, and meeting any one suffices. Here, the actual file move completes on the up-event, and releasing outside a valid target aborts the operation — this satisfies 'Abort or Undo.' Visual feedback on the down-event (highlighting) is not 'executing the function' in a meaningful sense; the function is the file move, not the visual cue. Option A misinterprets visual feedback as function execution. Option B is wrong because 2.5.2 applies to all single-pointer functionality, including the pointer aspects of drag-and-drop; 2.5.7 addresses the need for non-dragging alternatives, which is a separate concern.
learning_objective: Distinguish between visual feedback on the down-event and actual function execution under 2.5.2, and understand that abort-on-release satisfies the Abort or Undo provision.

wcag_extract: For user interface components with labels that include text or images of text, the name contains the text that is presented visually.
question: A form has a button that visually displays "Add to Cart". The developer sets aria-label="Add item to shopping cart" to be more descriptive for screen reader users. A voice-control user says "Click Add to Cart" and the command works. Does this satisfy 2.5.3?
choices: No, because 2.5.3 requires the accessible name to exactly match the visible text, and 'Add item to shopping cart' is not identical to 'Add to Cart'
Yes, because the accessible name 'Add item to shopping cart' contains the visible text 'Add to Cart' as a substring, which is what 2.5.3 requires
No, because 2.5.3 prohibits the use of aria-label when visible text is already present; the visible text must be the sole source of the accessible name
explanation: WCAG 2.5.3 requires that the accessible name 'contains the text that is presented visually.' It does not require an exact match — the visible text must be included within the accessible name. Since 'Add item to shopping cart' contains 'Add to Cart,' the criterion is satisfied. Option A is wrong because 2.5.3 requires containment, not identity. Option C is wrong because 2.5.3 does not prohibit aria-label; it only requires that whatever the accessible name ends up being, it includes the visible label text.
learning_objective: Understand that 2.5.3 requires the accessible name to contain (not exactly match) the visible text, allowing aria-label to extend the name as long as the visible text is preserved within it.

wcag_extract: Functionality that can be operated by device motion or user motion can also be operated by user interface components and responding to the motion can be disabled to prevent accidental actuation, except when the motion is used to operate functionality through an accessibility supported interface, or the motion is essential for the function and doing so would invalidate the activity.
question: A mobile banking app lets users tilt their device to reveal their account balance (a "peek" feature for privacy). The app also has a "Show Balance" button that achieves the same result. However, there is no setting to disable the tilt-to-peek motion response. A user with a tremor accidentally reveals their balance in public. Does this satisfy 2.5.4?
choices: Yes, because the Show Balance button provides a UI component alternative, which is the only requirement of 2.5.4
No, because 2.5.4 requires both a UI component alternative and a mechanism to disable the motion response to prevent accidental actuation; having only the button is insufficient
No, because 2.5.4 prohibits motion-based features in applications that handle sensitive data like banking, regardless of whether alternatives exist
explanation: WCAG 2.5.4 has two requirements: functionality operated by motion must also be operable via UI components, AND responding to motion can be disabled to prevent accidental actuation. The banking app meets the first requirement (the button) but fails the second (no way to disable tilt-to-peek). Option A is wrong because it addresses only half of 2.5.4's requirements. Option C is wrong because 2.5.4 does not categorically prohibit motion features based on content sensitivity; it requires alternatives and disabling mechanisms regardless of the domain.
learning_objective: Recognize that 2.5.4 imposes two separate obligations — a UI alternative AND a disable mechanism — and that satisfying only one is insufficient for conformance.

wcag_extract: All functionality that uses a dragging movement for operation can be achieved by a single pointer without dragging, unless dragging is essential or the functionality is determined by the user agent and not modified by the author.
question: An online drawing application requires users to drag to create freehand sketches as its core purpose. The developer provides no single-pointer alternative for the drawing function, arguing dragging is essential. A tester disagrees, saying a click-to-place-points polygon tool would satisfy 2.5.7. Who is correct?
choices: The tester, because 2.5.7 never permits the essential exception; a non-dragging alternative must always be provided regardless of the nature of the application
The developer, because freehand drawing inherently depends on the path of movement, making dragging essential to the function, and the essential exception in 2.5.7 applies
Neither, because 2.5.7 only applies to drag-and-drop operations that reposition objects, not to drawing or path-creation tools
explanation: WCAG 2.5.7 includes an explicit essential exception: when dragging is essential to the functionality, no alternative is required. Freehand drawing depends on the continuous path of the user's movement, making dragging essential. The first option incorrectly denies the existence of the essential exception, which is stated directly in the normative text. The third option fabricates a scope limitation; 2.5.7 covers all dragging movements, not just repositioning objects, but the essential exception still applies when the path-based input is fundamental to the activity.
learning_objective: Recognize when the essential exception in 2.5.7 legitimately applies, understanding that path-dependent drawing is one of the rare cases where dragging truly is essential to the function.

wcag_extract: The size of the target for pointer inputs is at least 24 by 24 CSS pixels, except when: Spacing (undersized targets are positioned so that if a 24 CSS pixel diameter circle is centered on the bounding box of each, the circles do not intersect another target or the circle for another undersized target); Equivalent (the function can be achieved through a different control on the same page that meets this criterion); Inline (the target is in a sentence or its size is otherwise constrained by the line-height of non-target text); User Agent Control (the size of the target is determined by the user agent and is not modified by the author); Essential (a particular presentation of the target is essential or is legally required for the information being conveyed).
question: A settings page has a 16 by 16 CSS pixel gear icon that opens a preferences panel. Elsewhere on the same page, a clearly labeled "Preferences" button (40 by 40 CSS pixels) opens the identical panel. Does the small gear icon violate WCAG 2.5.8?
choices: Yes, because every interactive target must independently meet the 24 by 24 pixel minimum; the existence of another control is irrelevant
No, because 2.5.8 has an equivalent exception: an undersized target conforms when the same function can be achieved through a different control on the same page that meets the criterion
No, because 16 by 16 pixels is acceptable under 2.5.8 as long as the target has sufficient color contrast against its background
explanation: WCAG 2.5.8 includes an equivalent exception: if the same function can be achieved through a different control on the same page that itself meets the 24x24 pixel minimum, the undersized target is exempt. The 40x40 Preferences button provides that equivalent control. The first option ignores this exception entirely. The third option conflates target size requirements with color contrast requirements (1.4.11); contrast is a separate concern and does not create a target size exemption.
learning_objective: Understand the equivalent exception in 2.5.8: an undersized target is permitted when an equivalent, conforming control for the same function exists on the same page.

wcag_extract: The default human language of each web page can be programmatically determined.
question: A multilingual corporate website serves pages in English, German, and Japanese. Each page includes the correct lang attribute on the <html> element matching the page's primary language. However, the HTTP Content-Language header is set to "en" for all pages regardless of actual content. Does the site conform to WCAG 3.1.1?
choices: No, because 3.1.1 requires the HTTP Content-Language header and the lang attribute to match; a mismatch between the two fails the criterion
No, because 3.1.1 requires both server-level and markup-level language declarations to be present and correct
Yes, because 3.1.1 requires that the default human language can be programmatically determined, and the lang attribute on the <html> element satisfies this requirement
explanation: WCAG 3.1.1 requires that the default human language of each web page can be programmatically determined. The lang attribute on the <html> element is the standard mechanism for this, and when correctly set, it satisfies the criterion. The HTTP Content-Language header is a server-level metadata mechanism; 3.1.1 does not require it to match or even be present. The first option invents a matching requirement between HTTP headers and HTML attributes that does not exist in the criterion. The second option incorrectly mandates dual declarations; a single correct programmatic determination is sufficient.
learning_objective: Understand that 3.1.1 is satisfied when the default language is programmatically determinable through markup (the lang attribute), and that HTTP Content-Language header mismatches do not constitute a 3.1.1 failure.

wcag_extract: The human language of each passage or phrase in the content can be programmatically determined except for proper names, technical terms, words of indeterminate language, and words or phrases that have become part of the vernacular of the immediately surrounding text.
question: An English-language news website publishes an article about a Japanese politician named "Tanaka Hiroshi." The name appears multiple times without a lang="ja" attribute. A tester flags every instance as a 3.1.2 failure. Is the tester correct?
choices: Yes, because the name originates from Japanese and must be marked with lang="ja" so screen readers can attempt correct pronunciation
No, because 3.1.2 explicitly exempts proper names from the requirement to programmatically identify their language
No, because 3.1.2 only requires language markup for passages longer than a single sentence, and a name is too short to qualify
explanation: WCAG 3.1.2 explicitly exempts proper names from the requirement to identify the human language of passages or phrases. "Tanaka Hiroshi" is a proper name regardless of its linguistic origin. The first option ignores this exemption and incorrectly assumes all foreign-origin text needs language markup. The third option fabricates a sentence-length threshold; 3.1.2 has no minimum length requirement; its exceptions are categorical (proper names, technical terms, indeterminate language, vernacular), not length-based.
learning_objective: Apply the proper-name exemption in 3.1.2 and understand that it applies regardless of the name's linguistic origin, distinguishing it from the other three exception categories.

wcag_extract: When any user interface component receives focus, it does not initiate a change of context.
question: A form contains a "Country" dropdown. When a user tabs to it (giving it focus), a tooltip appears below it showing "Please select your country." The tooltip does not move focus, does not open a new window, and does not alter the page structure. Does this violate WCAG 3.2.1?
choices: Yes, because any visible change triggered by focus constitutes a change of context under 3.2.1
Yes, because showing content on focus is governed exclusively by 1.4.13, and if it fails 1.4.13's dismissibility requirement, it also fails 3.2.1
No, because displaying a tooltip on focus is a change of content, not a change of context; 3.2.1 only prohibits changes of context such as new windows, focus shifts, or significant page restructuring
explanation: WCAG 3.2.1 prohibits changes of context on focus. A change of context includes events like opening a new window, moving focus to a different component, or significantly altering the page content in a way that disorients the user. A non-intrusive tooltip that simply appears without moving focus or restructuring the page is a change of content, not context. The first option conflates any visible change with a change of context, which is too broad. The second option incorrectly ties 1.4.13 compliance to 3.2.1; these are independent criteria, and a tooltip that does not constitute a context change does not violate 3.2.1 regardless of its 1.4.13 status.
learning_objective: Distinguish between a change of content (e.g., showing a tooltip) and a change of context (e.g., opening a new window or moving focus) under 3.2.1, recognizing that not all visible changes on focus are context changes.

wcag_extract: Changing the setting of any user interface component does not automatically cause a change of context unless the user has been advised of the behavior before using the component.
question: A search results page updates the displayed results in real time as the user types into a search filter field. The results area refreshes below the filter, but focus remains in the text field, no new page loads, and the URL does not change. Does this violate WCAG 3.2.2?
choices: No, because dynamically updating content on the same page without moving focus or loading a new page is a change of content, not a change of context under 3.2.2
Yes, because any automatic page update triggered by user input constitutes a change of context that requires advance notice under 3.2.2
Yes, because 3.2.2 prohibits all real-time filtering; the results should only update after the user presses a submit button
explanation: WCAG 3.2.2 addresses automatic changes of context triggered by input. A change of context involves events like navigating to a new page, significantly restructuring the DOM, or moving focus unexpectedly. Updating search results in place while maintaining focus in the input field is a change of content, not a change of context. The second option misclassifies all page updates as context changes; dynamic content updates that do not disorient the user are not context changes. The third option fabricates an absolute prohibition on real-time filtering that does not exist in the criterion.
learning_objective: Distinguish between a change of content (in-place result filtering) and a change of context (page navigation, focus movement) when evaluating 3.2.2, recognizing that dynamic in-page updates are not inherently context changes.

wcag_extract: Navigational mechanisms that are repeated on multiple web pages within a set of web pages occur in the same relative order each time they are repeated, unless a change is initiated by the user.
question: A website has a main navigation with five items. On every page, the currently active page's navigation item is visually highlighted with bold text and an underline, but all five items remain in the same position and order. A tester claims this visual differentiation of the active item violates 3.2.3 because the navigation is not identical across pages. Is the tester correct?
choices: No, because 3.2.3 requires consistent relative order of repeated navigational mechanisms, not identical visual presentation; highlighting the active item does not change the order
Yes, because 3.2.3 requires that navigation be completely identical in appearance and behavior across all pages in the set
Yes, because changing the visual weight of an item alters its prominence, which effectively changes the relative order for screen reader users
explanation: WCAG 3.2.3 requires that repeated navigational mechanisms occur in the same relative order. Visually highlighting the current page does not change the order of the navigation items; all five links remain in the same sequence. The second option misreads the criterion as requiring identical visual presentation, but 3.2.3 governs relative order, not styling. The third option incorrectly claims that visual emphasis changes relative order for screen reader users; screen readers process the DOM order, not visual weight, so bold styling does not alter the navigation sequence.
learning_objective: Understand that 3.2.3 governs the relative order of navigation items, not their visual styling, and that highlighting the active page is a common, conformant navigation pattern.

wcag_extract: Components that have the same functionality within a set of web pages are identified consistently.
question: A web application uses a floppy disk icon labeled "Save" in its document editor and the same floppy disk icon labeled "Save" in its spreadsheet editor. However, a completely different section of the site uses a download-arrow icon labeled "Save" for downloading files to the user's device. A tester flags the download icon as a 3.2.4 violation. Is the tester correct?
choices: Yes, because all buttons labeled "Save" across the site must use the same icon to satisfy 3.2.4's consistent identification requirement
No, because saving a document in an editor and downloading a file are different functionalities; 3.2.4 only requires consistent identification for components with the same functionality
Yes, because 3.2.4 requires that components with the same label always perform the same action, so using "Save" for downloading violates the criterion
explanation: WCAG 3.2.4 requires consistent identification of components that have the same functionality. Saving an in-app document and downloading a file to the user's device are different functions, even though both use the label "Save." The criterion does not require that all identically labeled components be unified; it requires that same-function components be identified the same way. The first option extends the criterion beyond its scope by demanding icon consistency for different functions. The third option reverses the criterion's direction; 3.2.4 is about identifying same-function components consistently, not about mandating that same-labeled components must perform the same action (though using the same label for different functions may raise usability concerns outside 3.2.4's scope).
learning_objective: Understand that 3.2.4 requires consistent identification of same-functionality components, and that components performing genuinely different functions are not required to share or avoid the same label under this criterion.

wcag_extract: If a web page contains any of the following help mechanisms, and those mechanisms are repeated on multiple web pages within a set of web pages, they occur in the same order relative to other page content, unless a change is initiated by the user: human contact details; human contact mechanism; self-help option; a fully automated contact mechanism.
question: A website displays a phone number (human contact details) in the footer of every page. On most pages, the footer also includes a chatbot widget (fully automated contact mechanism) positioned after the phone number. On two pages, the chatbot widget is absent entirely; only the phone number remains. Does the removal of the chatbot on those two pages violate WCAG 3.2.6?
choices: Yes, because 3.2.6 requires all help mechanisms present on any page to be present on every page in the set
No, because 3.2.6 governs the relative order of help mechanisms when they are repeated; it does not require every help mechanism to appear on every page
Yes, because removing the chatbot changes the relative order of the remaining help mechanisms, violating 3.2.6's ordering requirement
explanation: WCAG 3.2.6 requires that help mechanisms occur in the same order relative to other page content when they are repeated across pages. It does not mandate that every help mechanism be present on every page. If the chatbot is absent on two pages, there is no ordering violation; the phone number that does appear is still in its consistent position. The first option misreads 3.2.6 as requiring universal presence, but the criterion is about consistent ordering of repeated mechanisms. The third option incorrectly claims that removing an item changes the relative order of remaining items; if only the phone number remains, its position relative to other page content is unchanged.
learning_objective: Understand that 3.2.6 governs the consistent ordering of help mechanisms when they are repeated, and does not require that all help mechanisms appear on every page in the set.

wcag_extract: Labels or instructions are provided when content requires user input.
question: A survey form uses radio buttons for a question but provides no visible text explaining what the radio group represents. The radio buttons are labeled "1" through "5" with no indication of what scale they represent or what is being rated. Which criterion is the primary concern?
choices: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships, because the radio buttons lack a programmatic grouping with a fieldset and legend
2.4.6 Headings and Labels, because the labels "1" through "5" do not describe the topic or purpose of the controls
3.3.2 Labels or Instructions, because the form requires user input but provides no label or instruction explaining what the radio group is asking
explanation: WCAG 3.3.2 requires labels or instructions when content requires user input. A radio group with only numeric labels and no context about what is being rated fails to provide the instructions users need to make an informed selection. The first option raises a valid but secondary concern; programmatic grouping (1.3.1) addresses structure, not the absence of meaningful labels or instructions for user input. The second option cites 2.4.6, which requires that labels describe their purpose; while the labels are indeed vague, the fundamental issue is the absence of any instruction for the input group, which is squarely 3.3.2's concern.
learning_objective: Recognize that 3.3.2 applies not only to text fields but to all input mechanisms including radio groups, and that meaningful context about what is being asked is part of the label-or-instruction requirement.

wcag_extract: If an input error is automatically detected and suggestions for correction are known, then the suggestions are provided to the user, unless it would jeopardize the security or purpose of the content.
question: A government form has a free-text "Reason for appeal" field with a 500-character limit. When a user submits text exceeding 500 characters, the form displays "Your text exceeds the maximum length" but does not indicate the current character count or how many characters to remove. The system knows the exact overage. A colleague says this is fine because 3.3.3 only applies to fields with constrained formats like dates and emails. Is the colleague correct?
choices: Yes, because 3.3.3 applies only to fields with a specific expected format; free-text fields cannot have 'known suggestions' since the content is open-ended
No, because the system knows a specific corrective action (reduce by N characters) and 3.3.3 requires that known suggestions be provided; the criterion is not limited to format-constrained fields
No, because this is a 3.3.2 violation for failing to display the character limit as a label before input, not a 3.3.3 issue about suggestions after error detection
explanation: WCAG 3.3.3 applies whenever an input error is detected and suggestions for correction are known. The system knows exactly how many characters exceed the limit, so it can suggest 'Remove at least 37 characters' or show the current count versus the maximum. The criterion is not restricted to format-specific fields. Choice A incorrectly narrows the scope of 3.3.3 to format-constrained inputs; any field where the system can identify a corrective action qualifies. Choice C raises a valid but separate concern: while showing the limit upfront is good practice under 3.3.2 (Labels or Instructions), the post-submission failure to suggest how to fix the overage is squarely a 3.3.3 issue.
learning_objective: Understand that 3.3.3's 'suggestions are known' condition applies broadly to any input error where the system can compute a corrective action, not just fields with rigid format constraints.

wcag_extract: For web pages that cause legal commitments or financial transactions for the user to occur, that modify or delete user-controllable data in data storage systems, or that submit user test responses, at least one of the following is true: Reversible (submissions are reversible); Checked (data entered by the user is checked for input errors and the user is provided an opportunity to correct them); Confirmed (a mechanism is available for reviewing, confirming, and correcting information before finalizing the submission).
question: An online certification exam allows test-takers to navigate between questions freely, but when they click "Submit Exam," all answers are immediately and irrevocably graded with no review page, no confirmation dialog, and no option to change answers after submission. The platform argues that 3.3.4 does not apply because no money changes hands at the submission step. Is this correct?
choices: Yes, because 3.3.4 only applies to legal commitments, financial transactions, and data deletion; an exam submission is an educational activity outside its scope
No, but the relevant criterion is 2.2.1 Timing Adjustable, because the exam should allow extra time rather than a review step
No, because 3.3.4 explicitly covers pages that submit user test responses; at least one safeguard (reversible, checked, or confirmed) must be provided before finalizing
explanation: WCAG 3.3.4 lists three trigger categories: legal commitments, financial transactions, and submissions of user test responses. An online certification exam is a clear case of submitting user test responses. The platform must provide at least one safeguard: allow the submission to be reversed, check for errors with an opportunity to correct, or offer a review/confirmation step. Choice A omits the 'user test responses' trigger that is explicitly stated in 3.3.4. Choice B cites 2.2.1 (Timing Adjustable), which addresses time limits on activities; while relevant to timed exams, it does not address the pre-submission review safeguard that 3.3.4 requires.
learning_objective: Recognize that 3.3.4 explicitly includes 'user test responses' as a trigger category alongside legal and financial contexts, requiring pre-submission safeguards for exams and assessments.

wcag_extract: Information previously entered by or provided to the user that is required to be entered again in the same process is either auto-populated, or available for the user to select. Except when re-entering the information is essential, or the information is required to ensure the security of the content, or previously entered information is no longer valid.
question: A flight booking process collects the traveler's passport number on step 2. On step 4, the form asks the traveler to re-enter the passport number because the airline's backend re-validates it against a government database and the earlier validation token has expired. No auto-population is offered. Does this violate WCAG 3.3.7?
choices: No, because 3.3.7 includes an exception when previously entered information is no longer valid; an expired validation token means the earlier entry cannot be reliably reused
Yes, because 3.3.7 requires the author to auto-populate or make previously entered data selectable regardless of backend validation architecture
No, because passport numbers are government-issued identifiers and 3.3.7 only applies to user-generated content like names and addresses
explanation: WCAG 3.3.7 includes three exceptions: re-entry is essential, re-entry is required for security, or previously entered information is no longer valid. When a backend validation token expires and the earlier passport number entry can no longer be treated as verified, the 'no longer valid' exception applies. Choice B ignores the exceptions entirely; 3.3.7 does not require auto-population when a legitimate exception applies. Choice C fabricates a distinction between government-issued and user-generated data that does not exist in the criterion's text; 3.3.7 applies to any information previously entered in the same process.
learning_objective: Identify the 'previously entered information is no longer valid' exception in 3.3.7 and understand that expired validation states can legitimately require re-entry.

wcag_extract: A cognitive function test (such as remembering a password or solving a puzzle) is not required for any step in an authentication process unless that step provides at least one of the following: Alternative (another authentication method that does not rely on a cognitive function test); Mechanism (a mechanism is available to assist the user in completing the cognitive function test); Object Recognition (the cognitive function test is to recognize objects); Personal Content (the cognitive function test is to identify non-text content the user provided to the website).
question: A two-factor authentication flow first accepts a password (with paste enabled) and then requires the user to enter a six-digit time-based one-time code from an authenticator app. The site does not allow pasting into the OTP field and offers no alternative second factor (no SMS, no push notification, no hardware key). Does this conform to WCAG 3.3.8?
choices: Yes, because the first step (password with paste) conforms, and one-time codes are not cognitive function tests since the user reads them from a device rather than memorizing them

No, because entering a one-time code requires transcribing information within a time limit, which is a cognitive function test; blocking paste removes the assisting mechanism and no alternative second factor exists
No, because 3.3.8 prohibits multi-step authentication entirely; any process with more than one step is a violation
explanation: WCAG 3.3.8 applies to every step in an authentication process. Entering a time-based one-time code involves transcribing a value that requires recall (reading, remembering, and typing it before it expires), which constitutes a cognitive function test. Allowing paste would serve as the mechanism exception by letting users copy the code from the authenticator app. Blocking paste removes that mechanism, and with no alternative second factor offered, this step fails 3.3.8. Choice A incorrectly claims OTP entry is not a cognitive function test; the user must remember and transcribe the code under time pressure. Choice C overstates the criterion; 3.3.8 does not prohibit multi-step authentication but requires each step to meet at least one of the four exceptions if it involves a cognitive function test.
learning_objective: Recognize that 3.3.8 applies to every step in a multi-factor authentication flow, including one-time code entry, and that blocking paste on an OTP field removes the mechanism that would otherwise satisfy the exception.

wcag_extract: In content implemented using markup languages, status messages can be programmatically determined through role or properties such that they can be presented to the user by assistive technologies without receiving focus.
question: A file-upload page shows a progress indicator that updates from "Uploading... 25%" to "Uploading... 100%" and finally displays "Upload complete" — all without moving focus away from the upload button. The progress container uses no ARIA live region, role, or other programmatic announcement. A screen reader user has no way to know the upload status unless they manually navigate to the progress text. Which assessment is correct?
choices: This violates 4.1.2 because the progress indicator is a user interface component that lacks a programmatically determined role and value
This is acceptable because progress updates are transient visual feedback, not status messages; 4.1.3 only covers final confirmation messages
This violates 4.1.3 because the progress and completion messages are status messages presented without receiving focus and must be programmatically determinable through role or properties
explanation: WCAG 4.1.3 requires that status messages — information updates presented to the user without receiving focus — can be programmatically determined. Progress indicators and completion confirmations that appear without focus changes are classic status messages. Using role="progressbar" with aria-valuenow, or role="status" / aria-live="polite" on the container, would satisfy this. Choice A cites 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value), which applies to interactive user interface components the user operates; a non-interactive progress display is not a component under 4.1.2's scope. Choice B incorrectly narrows 4.1.3 to final confirmations only; the criterion covers all status messages including progress states, busy indicators, and incremental updates.
learning_objective: Understand that progress indicators and incremental status updates are status messages under 4.1.3 and require programmatic determination, not just final success/error confirmations.

wcag_extract: Sign language interpretation is provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media.
question: A government agency publishes a prerecorded video announcement with embedded sign language interpretation. The interpreter uses British Sign Language (BSL), but the agency's primary audience speaks American Sign Language (ASL). A reviewer argues this fails 1.2.6 because the wrong sign language is used. Based strictly on the normative text of 1.2.6, is the reviewer correct?
choices: Yes, because 1.2.6 implicitly requires the sign language to match the spoken language's regional sign language equivalent

No, because 1.2.6 requires that sign language interpretation is provided but does not specify which sign language must be used; the choice of sign language is a best-practice decision, not a normative requirement of this criterion
Yes, because 1.2.6 requires sign language interpretation that presents equivalent information, and using a different sign language fails the equivalence standard
explanation: The normative text of WCAG 1.2.6 states that 'sign language interpretation is provided for all prerecorded audio content in synchronized media.' It does not mandate a specific sign language. While providing interpretation in the audience's primary sign language is a strong best practice, the normative text itself only requires that sign language interpretation be present. The first answer invents a regional-matching requirement. The third answer conflates 'equivalent information' language from other criteria with 1.2.6's simpler requirement.
learning_objective: Recognize that 1.2.6's normative text requires the provision of sign language interpretation without specifying which sign language, distinguishing normative requirements from best-practice guidance.

wcag_extract: Where pauses in foreground audio are insufficient to allow audio descriptions to convey the sense of the video, extended audio description is provided for all prerecorded video content in synchronized media.
question: A training platform provides extended audio description for a fast-paced safety video. The extended version pauses playback to insert descriptions, making a 10-minute video take 18 minutes to watch. A stakeholder objects that the pausing disrupts the viewing experience and requests the extended description be replaced with a separate text document describing the visuals. Under 1.2.7, is the text document an acceptable substitute?
choices: Yes, because a text document describing the visuals is a valid form of extended audio description under 1.2.7
Yes, because 1.2.7 allows any alternative that conveys the sense of the video, including text-based alternatives, when audio description is impractical
No, because 1.2.7 specifically requires extended audio description; a text document would address 1.2.8 (Media Alternative) but does not satisfy 1.2.7's audio description requirement
explanation: WCAG 1.2.7 specifically requires 'extended audio description' — an audio-based solution where the video pauses to allow additional spoken description. A text document describing visuals falls under 1.2.8 (Media Alternative), which is a separate criterion. The two criteria address different needs: 1.2.7 provides synchronized audio narration for people who cannot see the video, while 1.2.8 provides a readable text alternative. The first answer incorrectly categorizes text as audio description. The second answer invents a flexibility that does not exist in 1.2.7's normative text.
learning_objective: Distinguish between extended audio description (1.2.7, an audio-based synchronized solution) and a media alternative (1.2.8, a text-based document), understanding they are separate requirements that cannot substitute for each other.

wcag_extract: An alternative for time-based media is provided for all prerecorded synchronized media and for all prerecorded video-only media.
question: An e-learning platform provides a prerecorded lecture video (synchronized media) along with a text-based media alternative. The media alternative includes a full transcript of the spoken content but does not describe the diagrams, animations, or visual demonstrations shown in the video. Does this media alternative satisfy 1.2.8?
choices: Yes, because a media alternative under 1.2.8 only needs to capture the audio content; visual descriptions are handled by audio description under 1.2.5 and 1.2.7
No, because 1.2.8 requires a media alternative that covers the full time-based media experience, meaning it must describe both the audio and visual content to serve as a true alternative
Yes, because 1.2.8's media alternative is equivalent to a transcript, and transcripts by definition cover only spoken content
explanation: An 'alternative for time-based media' under 1.2.8 must serve as a comprehensive substitute for the media experience. For synchronized media, this means covering both the audio and visual information — including descriptions of diagrams, animations, and on-screen actions. A transcript limited to spoken content alone does not provide an equivalent alternative. The first answer incorrectly siloes visual content into other criteria; 1.2.8 is meant to be a standalone comprehensive alternative. The third answer incorrectly equates a media alternative with a speech-only transcript.
learning_objective: Understand that a 1.2.8 media alternative must encompass both audio and visual content of synchronized media, not merely transcribe spoken words.

wcag_extract: An alternative for time-based media that presents equivalent information for live audio-only content is provided.
question: A sports network streams a live audio-only commentary of a basketball game on its website. The network argues that because this is a live event with rapidly changing information, providing a real-time text alternative is technically infeasible, and therefore 1.2.9 does not apply. Is the network's reasoning correct?
choices: Yes, because 1.2.9 includes an implicit exception for live events where real-time transcription is not technically feasible
Yes, because live sports commentary is synchronized media covered by 1.2.4 (Captions, Live), not 1.2.9, which applies only to non-event audio like announcements
No, because 1.2.9 contains no feasibility exception; it requires an alternative for time-based media that presents equivalent information for all live audio-only content
explanation: WCAG 1.2.9 states unconditionally that 'an alternative for time-based media that presents equivalent information for live audio-only content is provided.' There is no exception for technical difficulty or rapidly changing content. The first answer invents a feasibility exception that does not exist in the normative text. The second answer incorrectly categorizes audio-only content as synchronized media; 1.2.4 applies to live synchronized media (audio with video), while 1.2.9 specifically targets audio-only streams.
learning_objective: Recognize that 1.2.9 provides no exceptions for technical difficulty and applies specifically to live audio-only content, distinct from 1.2.4's scope of live synchronized media.

wcag_extract: In content implemented using markup languages, the purpose of user interface components, icons, and regions can be programmatically determined.
question: A web application built with a JavaScript framework renders all its content via a <canvas> element, including buttons, navigation, and text regions. None of these are implemented using HTML markup elements. A tester claims 1.3.6 does not apply to this application. Is the tester correct?
choices: No, because 1.3.6 applies to all web content regardless of implementation technology; canvas-based applications must expose purpose through ARIA or fallback markup

Yes, because 1.3.6's scope is limited to 'content implemented using markup languages'; if the UI components, icons, and regions are rendered entirely within canvas without markup, the criterion's precondition is not met
No, because the HTML page itself is a markup language document, so any content displayed within it automatically falls under 1.3.6
explanation: WCAG 1.3.6 explicitly scopes itself to 'content implemented using markup languages.' If a web application renders its entire UI within a <canvas> element without using markup for its components, icons, or regions, the precondition for 1.3.6 is technically not met. However, this does not mean the application is accessible — other criteria like 4.1.2 still apply. The first answer ignores the explicit markup language precondition. The third answer conflates the outer HTML document with the canvas-rendered content that lacks semantic markup.
learning_objective: Understand that 1.3.6 contains a scoping precondition — 'content implemented using markup languages' — and recognize the implications when content is rendered outside of markup structures.

wcag_extract: The visual presentation of text and images of text has a contrast ratio of at least 7:1, except for Large Text (at least 4.5:1), Incidental text, and Logotypes.
question: A website renders a promotional banner as an image of text. The text in the image has a 5.8:1 contrast ratio against its background. The text size within the image is equivalent to 14px regular weight. Under 1.4.6, does this image of text pass?
choices: No, because 1.4.6 applies to both text and images of text; at 14px regular weight the text is not large, so it requires 7:1, and 5.8:1 falls short
Yes, because 1.4.6 applies only to live (real) text that can be resized; images of text are governed exclusively by 1.4.9
Yes, because 5.8:1 exceeds the 4.5:1 minimum required by 1.4.3, and 1.4.6 does not apply to images of text since they cannot be user-customized
explanation: WCAG 1.4.6 explicitly covers 'the visual presentation of text and images of text.' The criterion makes no distinction between live text and images of text for contrast purposes. At 14px regular weight, the text does not qualify as large text, so the full 7:1 ratio applies. The 5.8:1 ratio falls below this threshold. The second answer incorrectly excludes images of text from 1.4.6's scope. The third answer applies the AA threshold (1.4.3) instead of the AAA threshold (1.4.6).
learning_objective: Confirm that 1.4.6's enhanced contrast requirements apply equally to images of text and live text, not just to resizable text elements.

wcag_extract: For prerecorded audio-only content that contains primarily speech in the foreground, is not an audio CAPTCHA or audio logo, and is not vocalization intended to be primarily musical expression, at least one of the following is true: No Background, Turn Off, or 20 dB difference.
question: A banking website uses an audio CAPTCHA that plays spoken characters over loud background noise to prevent automated recognition. The background noise is only 8 dB below the speech, well under the 20 dB threshold. Does this violate WCAG 1.4.7?
choices: No, because audio CAPTCHAs are explicitly excluded from 1.4.7's scope; the criterion does not apply to them
Yes, because 1.4.7 applies to all prerecorded audio with speech, and 8 dB is far below the required 20 dB difference
No, but only because the CAPTCHA audio is live-generated rather than prerecorded, and 1.4.7 applies only to prerecorded content
